content: Is that good weather?

Whether this is "good" weather depends on what you're planning and your personal preferences! Here's a balanced take:

**The Good:**
- Temperatures are mild for March (15°C/59°F high is decent for early spring)
- Not freezing or uncomfortably cold
- Wednesday looks nicer with mostly sunny skies

**The Not-So-Good:**
- 48% chance of rain today isn't ideal
- Overcast conditions can feel gloomy
- Cool enough that you'll need layers

**Verdict:**
- **For sightseeing:** Decent! You can still explore the city, museums, and cafes comfortably with a jacket
- **For outdoor activities:** Fair, but you might want to wait for Wednesday's sunnier day
- **For photography:** The overcast light can actually be nice for certain shots
- **For comfort:** Typical Amsterdam spring weather – pack accordingly!

**Bottom line:** It's not *great* weather, but it's not bad either. Amsterdam is a wonderful city to explore rain or shine (there are plenty of cozy cafes and world-class museums!). If you have flexibility, Wednesday looks like the better day for outdoor activities.

What are you planning to do? That might help determine if this weather works for you! 🌦️

The user is on a website titled 'zed-industries/zed: Code at the speed of thought – Zed is a high-performance, multiplayer code editor from the creators of Atom and Tree-sitter.' and has a question about it. Here's the text content of the website: 
 
  
  
  title
  description
  
  
  
  
  Building Zed for Linux
  Guide to building zed for linux for Zed development.
  
  


Building Zed for Linux
Repository
Clone the Zed repository.
Dependencies


Install rustup


Install the necessary system libraries:
script/linux
If you prefer to install the system libraries manually, you can find the list of required packages in the script/linux file.


Linkers {#linker}
On Linux, Rust's default linker is LLVM's lld. Alternative linkers, especially Wild and Mold, can improve clean and incremental build times.
Zed currently uses Mold in CI because it is more mature. For local development, Wild is recommended because it is typically 5-20% faster than Mold.
These linkers can be installed with script/install-mold and script/install-wild.
To use Wild as your default, add these lines to your ~/.cargo/config.toml:
[target.x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu]
linker = "clang"
rustflags = ["-C", "link-arg=--ld-path=wild"]

[target.aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu]
linker = "clang"
rustflags = ["-C", "link-arg=--ld-path=wild"]
To use Mold as your default:
[target.'cfg(target_os = "linux")']
rustflags = ["-C", "link-arg=-fuse-ld=mold"]
Building from source
Once the dependencies are installed, you can build Zed using Cargo.
For a debug build of the editor:
cargo run
And to run the tests:
cargo test --workspace
In release mode, the primary user interface is the cli crate. You can run it in development with:
cargo run -p cli
Installing a development build
You can install a local build on your machine with:
./script/install-linux
This builds zed and the cli in release mode, installs the binary at ~/.local/bin/zed, and installs .desktop files to ~/.local/share.

Note: If you encounter linker errors similar to the following:
error: linking with `cc` failed: exit status: 1 ...
= note: /usr/bin/ld: /tmp/rustcISMaod/libaws_lc_sys-79f08eb6d32e546e.rlib(f8e4fd781484bd36-bcm.o): in function `aws_lc_0_25_0_handle_cpu_env':
          /aws-lc/crypto/fipsmodule/cpucap/cpu_intel.c:(.text.aws_lc_0_25_0_handle_cpu_env+0x63): undefined reference to `__isoc23_sscanf'
          /usr/bin/ld: /tmp/rustcISMaod/libaws_lc_sys-79f08eb6d32e546e.rlib(f8e4fd781484bd36-bcm.o): in function `pkey_rsa_ctrl_str':
          /aws-lc/crypto/fipsmodule/evp/p_rsa.c:741:(.text.pkey_rsa_ctrl_str+0x20d): undefined reference to `__isoc23_strtol'
          /usr/bin/ld: /aws-lc/crypto/fipsmodule/evp/p_rsa.c:752:(.text.pkey_rsa_ctrl_str+0x258): undefined reference to `__isoc23_strtol'
          collect2: error: ld returned 1 exit status
  = note: some `extern` functions couldn't be found; some native libraries may need to be installed or have their path specified
  = note: use the `-l` flag to specify native libraries to link
  = note: use the `cargo:rustc-link-lib` directive to specify the native libraries to link with Cargo (see https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/build-scripts.html#rustc-link-lib)
error: could not compile `remote_server` (bin "remote_server") due to 1 previous error
Cause:
This is caused by known bugs in aws-lc-rs (no GCC >= 14 support): FIPS fails to build with GCC >= 14
& GCC-14 - build failure for FIPS module
You can refer to linux: Linker error for remote_server when using script/install-linux for more information.
Workaround:
Set the remote server target to x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu like so export REMOTE_SERVER_TARGET=x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu; script/install-linux

Wayland & X11
Zed supports both X11 and Wayland. By default, we pick whichever we can find at runtime. If you're on Wayland and want to run in X11 mode, use the environment variable WAYLAND_DISPLAY=''.
Notes for packaging Zed
This section is for distribution maintainers packaging Zed.
Technical requirements
Zed has two main binaries:

You will need to build crates/cli and make its binary available in $PATH with the name zed.
You will need to build crates/zed and put it at $PATH/to/cli/../../libexec/zed-editor. For example, if you are going to put the cli at ~/.local/bin/zed put zed at ~/.local/libexec/zed-editor. As some linux distributions (notably Arch) discourage the use of libexec, you can also put this binary at $PATH/to/cli/../../lib/zed/zed-editor (e.g. ~/.local/lib/zed/zed-editor) instead.
If you are going to provide a .desktop file you can find a template in crates/zed/resources/zed.desktop.in, and use envsubst to populate it with the values required. This file should also be renamed to $APP_ID.desktop so that the file follows the FreeDesktop standards. You should also make this desktop file executable (chmod 755).
You will need to ensure that the necessary libraries are installed. You can get the current list by inspecting the built binary on your system.
For an example of a complete build script, see script/bundle-linux.
You can disable Zed's auto updates and provide instructions for users who try to update Zed manually by building (or running) Zed with the environment variable ZED_UPDATE_EXPLANATION. For example: ZED_UPDATE_EXPLANATION="Please use flatpak to update zed.".
Make sure to update the contents of the crates/zed/RELEASE_CHANNEL file to 'nightly', 'preview', or 'stable', with no newline. This will cause Zed to use the credentials manager to remember a user's login.

Other things to note
Zed moves quickly, and distribution maintainers often have different constraints and priorities. The points below describe current trade-offs:

Zed is a fast-moving project. We typically publish 2-3 builds per week to address reported issues and ship larger changes.
There are a couple of other zed binaries that may be present on Linux systems (1, 2). If you want to rename our CLI binary because of these issues, we suggest zedit, zeditor, or zed-cli.
Zed automatically installs versions of common developer tools, similar to rustup/rbenv/pyenv. This behavior is discussed here.
Users can install extensions locally and from zed-industries/extensions. Extensions may install additional tools such as language servers. Planned safety improvements are tracked here.
Zed connects to several online services by default (AI, telemetry, collaboration). AI and our telemetry can be disabled by your users with their zed settings or by patching our default settings file.
Because of the points above, Zed currently does not work well with sandboxes. See this discussion.

Flatpak

Zed's current Flatpak integration exits the sandbox on startup. Workflows that rely on Flatpak's sandboxing may not work as expected.

To build & install the Flatpak package locally follow the steps below:

Install Flatpak for your distribution as outlined here.
Run the script/flatpak/deps script to install the required dependencies.
Run script/flatpak/bundle-flatpak.
Now the package has been installed and has a bundle available at target/release/{app-id}.flatpak.

Memory profiling
heaptrack is quite useful for diagnosing memory leaks. To install it:
$ sudo apt install heaptrack heaptrack-gui
$ cargo install cargo-heaptrack
Then, to build and run Zed with the profiler attached:
$ cargo heaptrack -b zed
When this zed instance is exited, terminal output will include a command to run heaptrack_interpret to convert the *.raw.zst profile to a *.zst file which can be passed to heaptrack_gui for viewing.
Perf recording
How to get a flamegraph with resolved symbols from a running Zed instance.
Use this when Zed is using a lot of CPU. It is not useful for hangs.
During the incident


Find the PID (process ID) using:
ps -eo size,pid,comm | grep zed | sort | head -n 1 | cut -d ' ' -f 2
Or find the PID of zed-editor with the highest RAM usage in something
like htop/btop/top.


Install perf:
On Ubuntu (derivatives) run sudo apt install linux-tools.


Perf record:
Run sudo perf record -p <pid you just found>, wait a few seconds to gather data, then press Ctrl+C. You should now have a perf.data file.


Make the output file user owned:
run sudo chown $USER:$USER perf.data


Get build info:
Run zed again and type zed: about in the command pallet to get the exact commit.


The perf.data file can be sent to Zed together with the exact commit.
Later
This can be done by Zed staff.

Build Zed with symbols:
Check out the commit found previously and modify Cargo.toml.
Apply the following diff, then make a release build.

[profile.release]
-debug = "limited"
+debug = "full"


Add the symbols to the perf database:
perf buildid-cache -v -a <path to release zed binary>


Resolve the symbols from the db:
perf inject -i perf.data -o perf_with_symbols.data


Install flamegraph:
cargo install cargo-flamegraph


Render the flamegraph:
flamegraph --perfdata perf_with_symbols.data


Troubleshooting
Cargo errors claiming that a dependency is using unstable features
Try cargo clean and cargo build.
summarize

content:
# Summary: Building Zed for Linux

This is a developer guide for building the Zed code editor on Linux systems.

## Key Sections

### **Setup & Dependencies**
- Clone the Zed repository
- Install rustup and system libraries (via `script/linux` or manually)
- Configure linkers: **Wild** (recommended for local dev, 5-20% faster) or **Mold** (used in CI)

### **Building**
- Debug build: `cargo run`
- Tests: `cargo test --workspace`
- CLI in dev mode: `cargo run -p cli`
- Install locally: `./script/install-linux`

### **Known Issues**
- **GCC ≥ 14 linker errors** with aws-lc-rs (FIPS module bug)
- **Workaround**: `export REMOTE_SERVER_TARGET=x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu` before installing

### **Display Servers**
- Supports both **X11 and Wayland**
- Default: auto-detects at runtime
- Force X11 mode: `WAYLAND_DISPLAY=''`

### **For Distribution Maintainers**
- Two binaries needed: `zed` (CLI) and `zed-editor` (main UI)
- Desktop file template available in `crates/zed/resources/zed.desktop.in`
- Can disable auto-updates via `ZED_UPDATE_EXPLANATION` env variable
- Set release channel in `crates/zed/RELEASE_CHANNEL` (nightly/preview/stable)

### **Important Notes**
- Zed releases 2-3 builds per week (fast-moving project)
- Auto-installs developer tools (like rustup/rbenv)
- Extensions can install additional tools (language servers)
- Connects to online services by default (AI, telemetry, collaboration) - can be disabled
- **Doesn't work well with sandboxes**

### **Flatpak**
- Currently exits sandbox on startup (sandboxing may not work as expected)
- Build script available: `script/flatpak/bundle-flatpak`

### **Profiling & Debugging**
- **Memory**: Use `heaptrack` (`cargo heaptrack -b zed`)
- **CPU/Flamegraphs**: Use `perf` to record, then `cargo-flamegraph` to visualize

### **Troubleshooting**
- For Cargo unstable feature errors: run `cargo clean` then `cargo build`

The user is on a website titled 'eurora-labs/eurora' and has a question about it. Here's the text content of the website: 
 
Welcome to Eurora, a context aware, cross-platform, fully private and open source AI platform.
The primary goal of Eurora is to provide seamless OS integration for AI assistants and ensure full privacy for users all around the world by applying the highest standards set by the European Union. We have first-party support for local deployment and will extensive documentation for running this on your own home server.
We're a community driven project, and we welcome contributions from anyone who is interested in helping us build a better future for AI.
License
Eurora is fair-code distributed under the Sustainable Use License, with each version re-licensed as Apache-2.0 two years after its release.
Our primary goal is to provide the community with a powerful and extensible AI platform that can run on your own home hardware, while rivaling the industry leaders in terms of performance and features.
Main points of Sustainable Use License (SUL) are:

Source Available: Always visible source code
Self-Hostable: Deploy anywhere
Extensible: Add your own plugins and features

Installation
Please download the version for your platform on https://www.eurora-labs.com/
summarize

# Eurora Summary

**What is Eurora?**
Eurora is a context-aware, cross-platform, fully private, and open-source AI platform designed for seamless OS integration with AI assistants.

**Key Features:**
- **Privacy-First:** Built to meet European Union privacy standards
- **Local Deployment:** First-party support for running on your own home server/hardware
- **Community-Driven:** Open to contributions from anyone
- **Extensible:** Supports custom plugins and features

**License:**
- Distributed under the **Sustainable Use License (SUL)**
- Versions re-licensed as **Apache-2.0** two years after release
- Core principles: Source Available, Self-Hostable, Extensible

**Goal:**
Provide a powerful AI platform that can run on personal hardware while competing with industry leaders in performance and features.

**Installation:**
Available for download at https://www.eurora-labs.com/

---

In essence, Eurora is positioning itself as a privacy-focused, self-hostable alternative to cloud-based AI assistants, with strong emphasis on user control and European privacy standards.

**Eurora** is a context-aware, cross-platform, fully private, and open-source AI platform with the following key characteristics:

- Provide seamless OS integration for AI assistants
- Ensure full user privacy following European Union standards
- Enable local deployment on personal home hardware

|---------|-------------|
| **Privacy-First** | Follows highest EU privacy standards |
| **Self-Hostable** | Run on your own home server |
| **Open Source** | Source code always visible |

| **Cross-Platform** | Works on multiple operating systems |

- Distributed under the **Sustainable Use License (SUL)**
- Each version becomes **Apache-2.0** licensed 2 years after release
- Community-driven project welcoming contributions

### Installation
Available for download at: https://www.eurora-labs.com/

---

In essence, Eurora positions itself as a privacy-focused alternative to commercial AI platforms, allowing users to run powerful AI assistants locally on their own hardware while maintaining full control over their data.
